chapter.title: Temporal Poses
chapter.para: A static pose pose represents a 2D/3D point and an orientation. For 2D poses the orientation is composed of a .

The pose type serves as base type for defining the temporal pose type tpose. The tpose type has similar functionality as the temporal point type tgeompoint with the exception that it only considers two dimensions. Thus, most functions and operators described before for the tgeompoint type are also applicable for the tpose type. In addition, there are specific functions defined for the tpose type.

sect1.title: Static Poses
sect1.para: A pose value is a couple of the form (point,radius) where point is a geometric point and radius is a float value greater than or equal to zero representing a radius around the point expressed using the units of the SRID of the point. Examples of input of pose values are as follows:
Values of the pose type must satisfy several constraints so that they are well defined. Examples of incorrect pose type values are as follows.
We give next the functions and operators for the pose type.

listitem.para: Constructor for poses

sect2.title: Conversions
sect2.para: Values of the pose type can be converted to the geometry point type using an explicit CAST or using the :: notation as shown below.

listitem.para: Convert between a pose and a geometry point
Return the point
Return the rotation
Return the orientation
Round the point and the radius of the pose to the number of decimal places
Return the spatial reference identifier
Set the spatial reference identifier
Transform to a spatial reference identifier
The transform function specifies the transformation with a target SRID. An error is raised when the input pose has an unknown SRID (represented by 0).

The transformPipeline function specifies the transformation with a defined coordinate transformation pipeline represented with the following string format: urn:ogc:def:coordinateOperation:AUTHORITY::CODE. The SRID of the input pose is ignored, and the SRID of the output pose will be set to zero unless a value is provided via the optional to_srid parameter. As stated by the last parameter, the pipeline is executed by default in a forward direction; by setting the parameter to false, the pipeline is executed in the inverse direction.
listitem.programlisting: WITH test(pose, pipeline) AS ( SELECT pose 'Pose(SRID=4326;Point(4.3525 50.846667),1)', text 'urn:ogc:def:coordinateOperation:EPSG::16031' ) SELECT asEWKT(transformPipeline(transformPipeline(pose, pipeline, 4326), pipeline, 4326, false), 6) FROM test; -- SRID=4326;Pose(POINT(4.3525 50.846667),1)

sect2.title: Comparisons
sect2.para: The comparison operators (=, <, and so on) are available for poses. They compare first the points and then the radius of the arguments. Excepted the equality and inequality, the other comparison operators are not useful in the real world but allow B-tree indexes to be constructed on poses.

listitem.para: Traditional comparisons

sect1.title: Temporal Poses
sect1.para: The temporal pose type tpose allows to represent the movement of objects together with a circular radius around them. It corresponds to the temporal point type tgeompoint restricted to two-dimensional coordinates. As all the other temporal types it comes in trhee subtypes, namely, instant, sequence, and sequence set. Examples of tpose values in these subtypes are given next.

The temporal pose type accepts type modifiers (or typmod in PostgreSQL terminology) to specify the subtype and/or the spatial reference identifier (SRID). The possible values for the subtype are Instant, Sequence, and SequenceSet. The two arguments are optional and if any of them is not specified for a column, values of any subtype and/or SRID are allowed.

Temporal pose values of sequence or sequence set subtype are converted into a normal form so that equivalent values have identical representations. For this, consecutive instant values are merged when possible. Three consecutive instant values can be merged into two if the linear functions defining the evolution of values are the same. Examples of transformation into a normal form are as follows.

sect1.title: Validity of Temporal Poses
sect1.para: Temporal pose values must satisfy the constraints specified in so that they are well defined. An error is raised whenever one of these constraints are not satisfied. Examples of incorrect values are as follows.

We give next the functions and operators for temporal pose. Most functions and operators for temporal types described in the previous chapters can be applied for temporal pose types. Therefore, in the signatures of the functions, the notation base represents a pose and the notations ttype, tpoint, and tgeompoint also represent a tpose. Furthermore, the functions that have an argument of type geometry accept in addition an argument of type pose. To avoid redundancy, we only present next some examples of these functions and operators for temporal poses.

listitem.para: Return the Well-Known Text (WKT) or the Extended Well-Known Text (EWKT) representation
Return the Well-Known Binary (WKB), the Extended Well-Known Binary (EWKB) representation, or the Hexadecimal Extended Well-Known Binary (HexEWKB) representation
The result is encoded using either the little-endian (NDR) or the big-endian (XDR) encoding. If no encoding is specified, then the encoding of the machine is used.
Input from the Well-Known Text (WKT) representation or from the Extended Well-Known Text (EWKT) representation
Input from the Well-Known Binary (WKB) representation, from the Extended Well-Known Binary (EWKB) representation, or from the Hexadecimal Extended Well-Known Binary (HexEWKB) representation
Constructor for temporal poses having a constant value
Constructor for temporal poses of sequence subtype
Constructor for temporal poses of sequence set subtype
Construct a temporal pose from a temporal geometry point and a temporal float
The time frame of the result is the intersection of the time frames of the temporal point and the temporal float. If they do not intersect, a null value is returned

sect1.title: Conversions
sect1.para: A temporal pose value can be converted to and from a temporal geometry point. This can be done using an explicit CAST or using the :: notation. A null value is returned if any of the composing geometry point values cannot be converted into a pose value.

listitem.para: Convert a temporal pose to a temporal geometry point
Convert a temporal pose to a temporal float
Return the values
Return the points
Return the value at a timestamp
Transform a temporal pose to another subtype
Transform a temporal pose to another interpolation
Round the points and the radii of the temporal pose to the number of decimal places
Get the spatial reference identifier
Set the spatial reference identifier
Transform to a spatial reference identifier
The transform function specifies the transformation with a target SRID. An error is raised when the input temporal pose has an unknown SRID (represented by 0).

The transformPipeline function specifies the transformation with a defined coordinate transformation pipeline represented with the following string format: urn:ogc:def:coordinateOperation:AUTHORITY::CODE. The SRID of the input temporal pose is ignored, and the SRID of the output temporal pose will be set to zero unless a value is provided via the optional to_srid parameter. As stated by the last parameter, the pipeline is executed by default in a forward direction; by setting the parameter to false, the pipeline is executed in the inverse direction.
listitem.programlisting: WITH test(tpose, pipeline) AS ( SELECT tpose 'SRID=4326;{Pose(Point(4.3525 50.846667),1)@2001-01-01, Pose(Point(-0.1275 51.507222),2)@2001-01-02}', text 'urn:ogc:def:coordinateOperation:EPSG::16031' ) SELECT asEWKT(transformPipeline(transformPipeline(tpose, pipeline, 4326), pipeline, 4326, false), 6) FROM test; /* SRID=4326;{Pose(POINT(4.3525 50.846667),1)@2001-01-01, Pose(POINT(-0.1275 51.507222),2)@2001-01-02} */

listitem.para: TODO Return the area traversed by the temporal pose
Construct a spatiotemporal box from a pose and, optionally, a timestamp or a period
TODO Return the instant of the first temporal pose at which the two arguments are at the nearest distance
TODO Return the smallest distance ever between the two arguments
Function nearestApproachDistance has an associated operator |=| that can be used for doing nearest neightbor searches using a GiST index (see ).
TODO Return the line connecting the nearest approach point between the two arguments
The function will only return the first line that it finds if there are more than one
TODO Return the smallest distance ever between the two arguments
TODO Return the temporal distance
TODO Restrict to (the complement of) a set of values
TODO Restrict to (the complement of) a geometry
Traditional comparisons
Ever and always comparisons
Temporal comparisons
Topological operators
listitem.programlisting: SELECT tpose '[Pose(Point(1 1), 0.3)@2001-01-01, Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)@2001-01-02]' && pose 'Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)' -- true SELECT tpose '[Pose(Point(1 1), 0.3)@2001-01-01, Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)@2001-01-02]' @> stbox(pose 'Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)') -- true SELECT pose 'Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)'::geometry <@ tpose '[Pose(Point(1 1), 0.3)@2001-01-01, Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)@2001-01-02]' -- true SELECT tpose '[Pose(Point(1 1), 0.3)@2001-01-01, Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)@2001-01-03]' ~= tpose '[Pose(Point(1 1), 0.3)@2001-01-01, Pose(Point(1 1), 0.35)@2001-01-02, Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)@2001-01-03]' -- true

listitem.para: Position operators
listitem.programlisting: SELECT tpose '[Pose(Point(1 1), 0.3)@2001-01-01, Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)@2001-01-02]' << pose 'Pose(Point(1 1), 0.2)' -- false SELECT tpose '[Pose(Point(1 1), 0.3)@2001-01-01, Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)@2001-01-02]' <<| stbox(pose 'Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)') -- false SELECT tpose '[Pose(Point(1 1), 0.3)@2001-01-01, Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)@2001-01-02]' &> pose 'Pose(Point(1 1), 0.3)'::geometry -- true SELECT tpose '[Pose(Point(1 1), 0.3)@2001-01-01, Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)@2001-01-02]' >># tpose '[Pose(Point(1 1), 0.3)@2001-01-03, Pose(Point(1 1), 0.5)@2001-01-05]' -- true

listitem.para: TODO Ever and always spatial relationships
TODO Temporal spatial relationships

sect1.title: Aggregations
sect1.para: The three aggregate functions for temporal poses are illustrated next.

listitem.para: Temporal count
Window count
TODO Temporal centroid

sect1.title: Indexing
sect1.para: GiST and SP-GiST indexes can be created for table columns of temporal poses. An example of index creation is follows:
sect1.programlisting: CREATE INDEX Trips_Trip_SPGist_Idx ON Trips USING SPGist(Trip);
sect1.para: The GiST and SP-GiST indexes store the bounding box for the temporal poses, which is an stbox and thus stores the absolute coordinates of the underlying space.
A GiST or SP-GiST index can accelerate queries involving the following operators:

listitem.para: <<, &<, &>, >>, <<|, &<|, |&>, |>>, which only consider the spatial dimension in temporal poses,
<<#, &<#, #&>, #>>, which only consider the time dimension in temporal poses,
&&, @>, <@, ~=, -|-, and |=| , which consider as many dimensions as they are shared by the indexed column and the query argument.

sect1.para: These operators work on bounding boxes, not the entire values.
